title: maktaba-web-local: Self-Calibrating Per-Tenant Relevance Gating for Offline Multilingual RAG

creators.name: Yousef, Ayman Kazim
creators.affiliation: Department of Artificial Intelligence Engineering, AlSafwa University, Karbala, Iraq

description: A retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system must decide, after retrieval and reranking, whether any candidate passage is relevant enough to ground an answer; otherwise it should answer from the model's general knowledge rather than forcing an off-topic document into the prompt. In practice this rests on a single hand-tuned global score cutoff, which is brittle: a reranker's score distribution shifts with the corpus, the language (multilingual cross-encoders score equally-relevant Arabic passages lower than English ones), and — in a multi-tenant system where every user owns a different private library — the tenant. We present an unsupervised, label-free, per-tenant self-calibrating relevance gate for the post-reranking stage of an offline, on-device, multilingual (Arabic+English) digital-library RAG system. The threshold is derived entirely from the relevance scorer's own score geometry — an in-domain self-match anchor versus a cross-document out-domain anchor — so it requires no user feedback, training, reward, or labeled evaluation set, works cold-start, and is computed on-device from local data only. A conditional cross-lingual fallback translates the query exactly once, only on a calibrated same-language miss. On a real 3,739-chunk, 10-book corpus on CPU, the label-free gate reaches F1 0.968 versus 0.828 for the deployable fixed default (precision 0.71→0.94, recall 1.0). The system and experiment scripts are released for reproduction. This archive also includes three companion papers (multi-tenant sparse-retrieval starvation; a continual label-free cross-script glossary; safe deterministic Iraqi-dialect rewriting). Code is MIT-licensed; the papers and prose are CC BY 4.0. The private evaluation corpus (user-owned books) is not redistributed.
